skill.name: remotion-to-hyperframes
skill.description: Converts existing Remotion video compositions into HyperFrames HTML, CSS, and GSAP timelines when the source follows supported frame-driven patterns.
skill.summary: Translate supported Remotion compositions into HyperFrames HTML with validation guidance.
skill.author: heygen-com

security_audit.summary: The static scan produced many hits, but most markdown backtick, path traversal, weak crypto, and Windows SAM alerts are false positives from documentation, expected JSON text, and variable names. Real risk remains because the skill ships shell and Python validation harnesses that run npm, npx, node, ffmpeg, ffprobe, and subprocess commands. I found no evidence of credential exfiltration, prompt injection, or confirmed malicious intent, so this is not a critical block.

high_findings.title: Validation Harness Executes Local Toolchains
high_findings.description: The test corpus runner can install npm dependencies and run Remotion, HyperFrames, node, ffmpeg, and shell scripts. This is expected for video translation validation, but marketplace users should treat it as executable build tooling and run it only in a controlled workspace.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines directly execute npm, npx Remotion, node HyperFrames, and local diff scripts. The behavior is legitimate validation work, but it is real command execution with dependency and media-processing risk.

medium_findings.title: Video Diff Scripts Process User-Supplied Files
medium_findings.description: The frame-strip and render-diff utilities pass provided video paths to ffprobe and ffmpeg through subprocess calls. Arguments are structured arrays or quoted shell variables, so command injection evidence is low, but malformed media can still exercise native parsers.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The subprocess calls are visible and operate on caller-provided media files. They do not concatenate shell commands, which lowers injection risk, but the native toolchain exposure is still meaningful.

medium_findings.title: Network Examples Are Present In Fixtures
medium_findings.description: The repository contains fixture code with fetch calls and CDN-hosted GSAP. These appear to be examples for lint detection and rendered fixtures, not hidden data exfiltration, but they can create outbound network behavior if executed in an unrestricted environment.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The fetch and external script URL are explicit. Context indicates fixture coverage and standard CDN use, so confidence is high for network behavior but lower for malicious intent.

low_findings.title: Static Analyzer Reported Many Documentation False Positives
low_findings.description: Most external command alerts in markdown files are inline code examples, API mapping tables, or fenced shell snippets. They are not executed by the skill unless a user intentionally follows the documentation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are documentation and markdown tables, not executable source files. They explain validation commands and translation mappings.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto Alerts Are SSIM Metric Text
low_findings.description: Weak cryptographic algorithm alerts on expected JSON and references are false positives. The cited expected file stores SSIM validation metrics and composition metadata, not hashing or encryption code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file content is video fixture metadata and measured SSIM values. No cryptographic operation is present in the inspected context.

low_findings.title: Prompt Injection Search Found No Evidence
low_findings.description: A targeted search for common prompt-injection phrases found no matching text in the skill files. No file content attempted to override audit instructions or claim special review authority.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The skill header and overview define the intended conversion scope. No suspicious override language was found during targeted repository search.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Orchestrator Runs Project Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The corpus runner launches dependency installation, rendering, diffing, and strip generation commands. This pattern is dangerous if run on untrusted projects without isolation.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The shell script directly launches multiple project commands. The commands are purposeful and local, but they should be sandboxed for marketplace use.

dangerous_patterns.title: Python Subprocess Invokes ffprobe And ffmpeg
dangerous_patterns.description: The frame-strip helper invokes ffprobe and ffmpeg from Python subprocesses using caller-supplied video paths. This is a real external command pattern with media parser exposure.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The code uses structured subprocess arguments, which mitigates shell injection. The external parser exposure is still confirmed.

content.value_statement: Teams often need to move frame-driven Remotion videos into HyperFrames without losing timing accuracy. This skill maps supported React video patterns to HTML, CSS, and GSAP timelines with validation guidance.
actual_capabilities: Maps Remotion Composition, AbsoluteFill, Sequence, and timing APIs to HyperFrames structures.
Guides conversion of useCurrentFrame, interpolate, spring, and color interpolation into GSAP timelines.
Detects unsupported Remotion patterns with a bundled lint script before translation.
Provides reference guides for parameters, sequencing, timing, media, transitions, Lottie, and fonts.
Includes fixture-based validation guidance using Remotion renders, HyperFrames renders, and SSIM comparison.
Recommends runtime interop when a Remotion project cannot translate safely.
limitations: It is intended only for explicit Remotion-to-HyperFrames migration requests.
It does not translate React state machines or side-effect-driven animation logic.
It may drop deployment-specific Remotion features such as Lambda rendering configuration.
It requires local render and diff tools for full visual validation.

use_cases.title: Migrate a Supported Video Component
use_cases.description: Convert a deterministic Remotion composition into HyperFrames HTML while preserving frame timing and layout.

use_cases.title: Assess Translation Feasibility
use_cases.description: Run the source lint workflow to decide whether a Remotion project should translate or use runtime interop.

use_cases.title: Validate Visual Parity
use_cases.description: Compare Remotion and HyperFrames renders with SSIM thresholds and inspect divergent frames.

prompt_templates.title: Check If My Remotion Project Can Convert
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the remotion-to-hyperframes skill to inspect this Remotion source and tell me whether it can be converted to HyperFrames.

prompt_templates.title: Convert A Simple Composition
prompt_templates.prompt: Convert this Remotion composition to a HyperFrames index.html file. Preserve duration, fps, dimensions, timing, and static props.

prompt_templates.title: Translate Timing And Media
prompt_templates.prompt: Port this Remotion video to HyperFrames. Pay special attention to Sequence timing, interpolate calls, media assets, and generated translation notes.

prompt_templates.title: Perform Full Validation
prompt_templates.prompt: Convert this Remotion project to HyperFrames, run the lint and render comparison workflow, and summarize any SSIM failures or unsupported patterns.

output_examples.input: A Remotion title card with AbsoluteFill, useCurrentFrame, and interpolate opacity.
output_examples.output: A HyperFrames HTML composition with a stage element, static title markup, CSS initial opacity, and a paused GSAP timeline.

output_examples.input: A Remotion project that uses useState and useEffect to fetch render-time data.
output_examples.output: A refusal to translate the composition directly, plus a recommendation to use runtime interop or resolve data before build time.

output_examples.input: A multi-scene Remotion composition with audio, images, and spring timing.
output_examples.output: A converted HyperFrames scene structure with asset path notes, timing mappings, and validation steps for render comparison.

best_practices: Run the lint workflow before writing any HyperFrames output.
Load only the reference guides that match APIs used by the source project.
Validate important migrations with matched render settings and SSIM comparison.
anti_patterns: Do not use this skill for brand-new HyperFrames authoring work.
Do not translate projects with state-driven animation as static HTML without warning.
Do not skip visual validation when timing, media, or transitions changed.

faq.question: When should I use this skill?
faq.answer: Use it when the user explicitly asks to port, convert, migrate, translate, or rewrite Remotion source as HyperFrames.

faq.question: Can it convert every Remotion project?
faq.answer: No. It refuses patterns that do not fit HyperFrames seek-driven rendering, such as React state machines and async metadata.

faq.question: What does the output usually include?
faq.answer: The expected output is HyperFrames HTML, CSS, GSAP timeline logic, and notes about unsupported or approximated behavior.

faq.question: Does it run code automatically?
faq.answer: The skill describes validation scripts that can run local commands. Run them only in a trusted or sandboxed workspace.

faq.question: How does it handle Remotion Lambda?
faq.answer: Lambda configuration is treated as deployment-specific. The skill drops it as a warning and translates the composition when possible.

faq.question: How is quality checked?
faq.answer: The references recommend rendering both versions with matching settings and comparing them with SSIM thresholds.
